title: The Redundancy Bay
subtitle: AND, OR, and independence
realWorldHook: One hydraulic system fails on 1 in 10 flights — scary. Bolt on two more independent backups and total failure drops to about 1 in 1,000.
conceptSummary: Independent failures multiply for AND (catastrophe gets rarer fast), but 'at least one fails' adds up for OR (maintenance gets more common).

steps.title: Two ways to build a safe plane
steps.body:
A passenger jet needs to almost never lose all hydraulic power. There are two ways to get there.

You could install **one really good system** that fails only 1 in 100 flights. Or you could bolt on **three cheaper backups**, each failing 1 in 10, and let the plane fly as long as **at least one** still works — because the systems fail [[independent|Two events are independent when one happening tells you nothing about the other — two separate hydraulic systems, sealed off from each other, fail on their own. For independent events, the chance they *all* fail is the product of their individual chances.]]ly of each other.

Which design is safer? You're about to fly the same fleet through both.

steps.prompt: Three independent backups, each failing 1 in 10 flights. How often do **all three** fail on the same flight?

options.label: About 1 in 30 — add the three chances up
Still about 1 in 10 — backups don't really help

steps.revealMessage: Lock that in — then fly the same fleet through both designs at once and see which is safer.

revealByOption.a: That's the gut answer, and it's the classic trap: independent failures **multiply**, they don't add. Fly the fleet and watch what the two grids do.
revealByOption.b: Bold call — and it's exactly right. Let's prove it against the single 1-in-100 system.
revealByOption.c: Backups help an astonishing amount — far more than intuition says. Let's see it.

steps.prompt: Fly the same **1,000 flights** through both designs and see which is safer. The left plane has **three backups, each failing 1 in 10**; the right has **one system failing 1 in 100**. Press *Fly the fleet*, then count the red crashes in each grid.

Now experiment with your own design. Drag the sliders to change how many backup systems you stack and how often each one fails — the live odds update as you go. Flip on **Compare** to put your plane next to a single 1-in-100 system, then *Fly the fleet*.

Recall how the two fleets compared: the triple backup (three systems each failing 1 in 10) crashed about *ten times less often* than the single system failing 1 in 100. Why are three mediocre backups safer than one better system?

options.label: Independent failure chances multiply: 1/10 × 1/10 × 1/10 = 1/1,000, far below the single system's 1/100
Three systems are simply three times stronger than one
The single system was just built badly; the redundancy didn't really matter

feedback.correct: Exactly — for an AND of independent events you multiply: 1/10 × 1/10 × 1/10 = 1/1,000, which is ten times rarer than the single system's 1/100. Stacking backups multiplies the safety.
feedback.incorrect: It's the multiplying. For all three independent systems to fail at once: 1/10 × 1/10 × 1/10 = 1/1,000 — ten times rarer than the single system's 1/100. Redundancy multiplies.

byOption.b: 'Three times stronger' is additive thinking. The real win is multiplicative: 1/10 × 1/10 × 1/10 = 1/1,000, which is ten times safer than the single 1-in-100 system, not three.
byOption.c: It's not about build quality — it's the math. Three [[independent|One system failing tells you nothing about the others; they don't help or fix one another. That's exactly why their failure chances multiply.]] backups only all fail together 1/10 × 1/10 × 1/10 = 1/1,000 of the time, beating a single 1-in-100 system by tenfold.

steps.hint: For an AND of independent events, do you add the three 1-in-10 chances or multiply them? Compare the result to 1 in 100.

steps.prompt: Recall how the two fleets compared: the triple-backup plane flagged *far more* flights for maintenance than the single system, even though it crashed far less. Why do crashes and maintenance flags move in opposite directions as you add parts?

options.label: 'At least one fails' (OR) adds up across more parts, while 'all fail' (AND) multiplies down — so more systems means more maintenance but rarer crashes
The backup systems are just lower quality than the single one
A crash and a maintenance flag are really the same event

feedback.correct: Exactly — 'all fail' (AND) shrinks fast, but 'at least one fails' (OR) grows with more parts. The triple backup is far safer from a crash, yet trips maintenance more often because there are simply more parts that can flag.
feedback.incorrect: Crashes are the AND of every system failing, which multiplies down. Maintenance is the OR — 'at least one failed' — and more parts means more chances for *something* to flag.

byOption.b: Each backup fails 1 in 10 — worse than the single 1-in-100 system, yes — but maintenance climbs because OR counts *any* single failure, and three parts give three ways for one to trip.
byOption.c: They're different events, and they're not [[mutually exclusive|Two events are mutually exclusive when they can't both happen at once. A crash (all systems failed) is actually a special case *inside* 'at least one failed', so it isn't separate from maintenance — it's the worst corner of it.]]: a crash is the rare corner of 'at least one failed', while most maintenance flags are flights that lost just one system and landed fine.

steps.hint: Crashes need *every* system to fail (AND). Maintenance needs only *one* (OR). Which one grows as you add parts?

steps.title: AND shrinks, OR grows
steps.body:
For independent events, **AND multiplies** — which is why stacking three rare failures makes catastrophe astronomically unlikely (1/10 × 1/10 × 1/10 = 1/1,000).

**OR** is 'at least one' — and it *grows* with more parts, because every extra system is one more thing that can flag for maintenance.

That's the engineer's trade: more redundancy buys you dramatically safer flights at the cost of more frequent — but harmless — maintenance. Next: what happens to a probability once you *know* something.
